D7 Print L5 Evaluate Reference

Goniochromic Multilayer Stacks

stacking many quarter-wave layers turns a faint tint into a saturated, angle-travelling colour — the engine behind security flakes

A single film gives a weak, broad colour. A distributed Bragg reflector stacks many high/low-index quarter-wave layers so their reflections add in phase at one wavelength — a tall, narrow peak. More layers means a purer, brighter colour; tilting slides that peak across the spectrum, so the flake travels through many hues, not just two.

Add layers and watch the reflectance peak narrow and the swatch saturate. Tilt to sweep the hue. (The peak is approximated as a band that narrows with layer count; a real DBR transfer-matrix gives the same envelope.)
